transcript:
What you're watching is a camera that doesn't belong to you — it's drifting on its own schedule through a field of thousands of glowing spheres, each one being pulled toward one of six invisible gravity wells that drift through the dark independently.

Here's the thing: in the function called "evolve the kept," every single sphere gets updated identically whether it's in frame or not — there's no branch that says "skip this if the camera can't see it." Compare that to "leave attention marks," where those little amber crystals only spawn near wherever the camera happens to be looking — that's attention writing a mark on the world, but never causing the world's motion. Even the warmup, before the first frame renders, runs eighteen physics steps so you arrive mid-process, never at a true beginning.

So the camera panning away and back isn't really "leaving" and "returning" — it's just attention catching up to a story that was writing itself the entire time it looked elsewhere.
